dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, two units for possible respiratory distress at a residence in Richmond. Call Type: Sick Person.
Hi, my name is June, I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. I see you're in your home. What made you call 911 today?
I've been very tired and can't seem to catch my breath for over an hour now.
I understand. We'll take good care of you. How have these symptoms been affecting you?
Madame, can you tell me your name?
Yes, it's Elizabeth.
Can you tell me where you're today?
I think I'm in Richmond... I'm not sure.
Do you know what time it is today?
I'm not really sure. Maybe 8-something?
You're alert and talking. Is there any discomfort in your throat or neck? Are you choking or having trouble speaking?
No. I can talk just fine, but it does take a lot of effort.
Can I check your breathing now, just to be sure?
Respiration rate is 24 breaths per minute. Your chest moves well and is equal on both sides.
Were you wheezing or having a tight chest sensation?
Yes, I had some tightness in my chest when I breathe.
Let me check your pulse and blood pressure. Is that okay?
Your pulse is 87 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 89 over 60.
Your oxygen level is 92 with room air. We'll check that again once we add more oxygen.
Madam, could you tell me your age?
You mentioned feeling tired and short of breath. Any other symptoms today?
Sometimes I feel a bit lightheaded when I lie down. But now I just feel like I can't take a deep enough breath.
Have you had any reactions to medications or allergens?
I'm allergic to latex and some medications like Sulfa.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Albuterol, Apixaban, atorvastatin, Furosemide, and others.
I see you've had a lot of previous health issues. Could you please share your medical history?
I have COPD, asthma, heart problems, diabetes, and high cholesterol. I also have high blood pressure.
You mentioned heart problems. Have you ever been diagnosed with congestive heart failure?
Yes. I have CHF as well.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst you've ever felt, how would you rate your breathing difficulty?
Maybe a 6.
I'll listen to your lungs to assess breathing sounds.
Your breathing is wheezy and somewhat difficult. We may need some medication to improve that. Can you take that?
Your skin looks warm and dry with healthy color. There are no visible rashes or bruising.
Your head and neck appear normal. No masses or pain if I touch your neck.
Your throat and nose are clear. I'll check your mouth with this small light.
Your chest rises and falls equally. I heard wheezing in both lungs.
Your limbs appear normal in color. Your pulses are strong. Any pain or swelling?
No. My legs just feel heavy.
Your ECG is abnormal and shows rapid atrial flutter. This might be why you're not breathing well.
In addition to the standard rhythm, I've placed you on a 12-lead ECG. This gives us a more detailed picture of your heart.
We're also running a 4-lead continuous ECG monitor to keep track of your heart rhythm during transport.
After the nebulizer, your blood pressure is a bit higher: 109 over 67.
Heart rate decreased to 75, but it is still irregular.
Respiration rate is 21 now, which is a slight drop.
Oxygen improved to 100 percent while on 4 liters of oxygen.
We're going to give you some nebulizer medication to help you breathe. It's Albuterol and Atrovent, and you'll feel it warm in your throat.
Your blood pressure now is 94 over 58.
Heart rate increased to 98 beats, still irregular.
Respirations spiked to 26.
Oxygen is now at 92 percent with 10 liters on.
We're going to take another set of vitals to check how you're responding to treatment.
Pulse is 104 now, still irregular.
Respirations remain at 26.
Blood pressure has improved a bit to 124 over 83.
Oxygen is at 99 percent with 10 liters.
EKG again shows rapid atrial flutter. It remains inconsistent.
We're now going to give you another nebulizer treatment. It might help after a short rest.
After medication, heart rate is now at 110.
EKG is still atrial flutter.
Oxygen levels improved to 100 percent on 10 liters with nebulizer.
Based on your symptoms and test results, we'll follow the respiratory distress protocol for asthma and COPD.
I believe we're taking you to St. Mary's Hospital. Is that okay with you?
Yes, that's fine.
We're going to move you now for transport while securing you onto a stretcher. Is that okay?
Okay. Be quick, please.
We're going to use a 5-point belt harness to keep you safe.
I'm placing your IV site in a stable position for transport. We couldn't place it successfully here, but it's secure for now.
We'll recheck your GCS now. Can you answer a few more questions?
EMT, let me confirm. Eye, verbal, and motor responses look consistent with a GCS of 15.
I'm not able to test your glucose right now.
Your overall pain level remains at 0 across all activities.
We're going to transfer you using a sheet lift. I'll make sure the process is smooth and stable.
We called ahead to St. Mary's. The ER is aware of your condition and the medications you received on scene.
Your IV wasn't successful here, but we left it ready for the hospital in case they can place it there.
We're ready to go, but I just want to confirm your comfort before we roll you out the door.
Yep, just do what you need. I appreciate the work you've done.
Before we take off, I'll check your breathing and oxygen levels one last time.
Your respirations are 19, equal and consistent.
Blood pressure is 149 over 133 now.
I'll be securing your stretcher to the ambulance stretcher while you're being transferred.
We should be able to get to the hospital safely in time for them to take over.
Hospital bed is ready, and we'll transfer you onto it by sheet lift.
I'll be handing over patient care to the hospital nurse.
You've been very good with me. I feel a lot better now, actually.
We're glad to hear that. Sleep now, we're almost there.
We entrust your care to the hospital team at St. Mary's.
Vital signs and interventions prior to transfer were recorded and will be relayed in our handoff report.
Respiration rate remains at 19.
One of us will remain with you on board the hospital stretcher during the transfer.
All medications, vitals, assessments, and procedures have been documented and are ready for your review.
Your IV was also established on scene and not attained again. Your IV site remains stable.
